keyPoints: S - Shared Memory is a region of memory that can be accessed by multiple processes.
E - Everyone has a unique Process ID (PID) which is assigned by the operating system.
E - Exchanging data between processes can be done through the use of file descriptors.
C - Client-server architecture is a common pattern in networked systems.
L - Limitations of shared memory include data consistency and security.
E - Ensuring data consistency and security in a client-server system.
A - Access to shared memory can be made through the use of file descriptors.
R - Reliable data transfer between processes can be achieved through the use of sockets.
C - Client and server roles can be separated for easier maintenance.
L - Limitations of client-server architecture include scalability and fault tolerance.
E - Ensuring scalability and fault tolerance in a client-server system.
A - Asynchronous communication can be used in client-server systems.
